items.headline: OpenAI sees security hole in third-party tool; no access to user data
items.whatHappened: OpenAI said Friday that it has identified a security issue with a third-party developer tool, Axios, and is taking steps to protect the process that certifies its macOS applications as legitimate OpenAI apps.
items.whyItMatters: This may influence AI product strategy, regulation, or competitive positioning in Europe and beyond.

links.title: OpenAI sees security hole in third-party tool; no access to user data

items.headline: The Netherlands is the first country in Europe to allow self-driving software from Tesla
items.whatHappened: The software allows drivers to hit the road without keeping their hands on the wheel. The RDW will submit an application to also allow the system in the rest of the European Union.
items.whyItMatters: It signals a broader shift in the European tech landscape, with potential effects on product direction, competition, or regulation.

links.title: The Netherlands is the first country in Europe to allow self-driving software from Tesla

items.headline: How a Dutch CEO fought his Chinese bosses with EZ

items.headline: Tesla receives Dutch approval for self-driving function
items.whatHappened: It is the first time that the company has received approval for the driver assistance system in Europe. It will now be available in phases, at €99 per month, for Dutch Tesla owners.

items.headline: Why the Senate is so relevant now

items.headline: The Woutertje Pieterse Prize goes to the enigmatic 'Atman!' by Bart Moeyaert and Mark Janssen
items.whatHappened: The Woutertje Pieterse Prize for the best children's book has been awarded to writer Bart Moeyaert and illustrator Mark Janssen for their book 'Atman!'. The jury praised “the power of oral storytelling, the music in language, the singing of words and images.”
items.whyItMatters: This story has practical implications for business and policy watchers following Dutch and global developments.

links.title: The Woutertje Pieterse Prize goes to the enigmatic 'Atman!' by Bart Moeyaert and Mark Janssen
